name: EGCG (Green Tea)
historicalUse: Green tea has been consumed as both a beverage and a medicine in China and Japan for over 4,000 years. It appears in the Shennong Bencao Jing as a remedy for fatigue, mental alertness, and detoxification. Japanese Zen Buddhist monks used it to maintain alertness during long meditation sessions. Arabic physicians who encountered it through trade routes documented its stimulant and digestive properties. The specific catechin EGCG was isolated in the 20th century but the plant's medicinal use spans millennia.
classicalSources: Chinese Materia Medica (Bencao Gangmu, Li Shizhen)
modernEvidence: Epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG) is the most abundant and bioactive catechin in green tea, present at 98% in highly standardized extracts. It is one of the most studied polyphenols in nutritional science with over 5,000 published studies. Key documented activities include inhibition of cancer cell proliferation (multiple pathways), cardiovascular protection, metabolic support, and neuroprotection. At 98% standardization, dosing precision and efficacy are maximized versus crude extracts.
mechanisms: AMPK activation — improves cellular energy sensing and metabolic flexibility
Nrf2 pathway activation — upregulates endogenous antioxidant enzyme systems
EGFR and VEGF inhibition — modulates cellular growth factor signaling
Proteasome inhibition — supports cellular protein quality control
NF-kB suppression — reduces inflammatory cytokine transcription
benefits: Supports cellular antioxidant defense at the molecular level
Supports healthy metabolic function and energy balance
safetyProfile: Well tolerated at standard doses. High-dose isolated EGCG (>800mg/day) has been associated with rare hepatotoxicity — primarily from concentrated supplements taken on empty stomach. At recommended doses with meals, risk is minimal. Green tea EGCG has superior safety to some other concentrated polyphenols.
contraindications: Liver disease — high-dose concentrated EGCG with caution; monitor LFTs
Iron deficiency anemia — EGCG reduces non-heme iron absorption
Pregnancy at high doses — caffeine content (if present) and high-dose catechins not recommended
drugInteractions: Warfarin — EGCG may reduce anticoagulant effect (contains vitamin K activity); monitor INR
Iron supplements — reduce absorption; separate doses by 2+ hours
Bortezomib (cancer medication) — EGCG may inhibit this proteasome inhibitor; avoid combination
Stimulant medications — if caffeine is present in extract, additive CNS stimulation
physicianNotes: Specify that this formulation uses decaffeinated/caffeine-free EGCG for patient tolerability. High-dose isolated EGCG should always be taken with food to reduce hepatotoxicity risk. Monitor liver function in patients taking concentrated EGCG long-term. In oncology contexts, the EGCG-bortezomib interaction is clinically significant — avoid in patients on this regimen.
patientGuidance: Take with food — never on an empty stomach at concentrated doses. If you have liver conditions or take blood thinners, inform your physician. Separate from iron supplements by at least 2 hours.
